edges.description: Reduced RAI1 dosage lowers transcription of CLOCK and other core clock genes, dysregulating the molecular circadian oscillator.
RAI1 haploinsufficiency disrupts hypothalamic feeding and satiety signaling, contributing to hyperphagia and obesity.
The 17p11.2 deletion removes one RAI1 allele, producing RAI1 haploinsufficiency as the central downstream molecular consequence that drives the core SMS phenotype.
Dysregulation of the molecular circadian clock drives the inverted timing of pineal melatonin secretion.

nodes.description: Childhood-onset abdominal obesity is a recognized feature of SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: childhood-onset abdominal obesity

nodes.description: Congenital heart defects, including tetralogy of Fallot and pulmonary stenosis, occur in a subset of individuals with SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome: Clinical and behavioral characteristics in a large retrospective cohort.
evidence.snippet: Prevalence of heart defects (6.5% tetralogy of Fallot, 6.5% pulmonary stenosis)

nodes.description: SMS is characterized by an inverted circadian rhythm of melatonin secretion, with secretion peaking during the day rather than at night.

evidence.reference_title: Inversion of the circadian rhythm of melatonin in the Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: All children with SMS had a phase shift of their circadian rhythm of melatonin.

nodes.description: Maladaptive behaviors include aggression, oppositional behavior, and frequent temper tantrums/outbursts.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: opposition, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors

nodes.description: Significant anxiety is common in individuals with SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Significant anxiety is common as are problems with executive function

nodes.description: RAI1 is a positive transcriptional regulator of CLOCK, a core component of the mammalian circadian oscillator. RAI1 haploinsufficiency reduces CLOCK transcription and dysregulates downstream clock genes (PER2, PER3, CRY1, BMAL1), disrupting the molecular circadian rhythm. This underlies the inverted melatonin rhythm and abnormal sleep-wake cycle in SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome results in disruption of CLOCK gene transcription and reveals an integral role for RAI1 in the maintenance of circadian rhythmicity.
evidence.snippet: RAI1 regulates the transcription of circadian locomotor output cycles kaput (CLOCK), a key component of the mammalian circadian oscillator

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome results in disruption of CLOCK gene transcription and reveals an integral role for RAI1 in the maintenance of circadian rhythmicity.
evidence.snippet: haploinsufficiency of RAI1 and Rai1 in SMS fibroblasts and the mouse hypothalamus, respectively, results in the transcriptional dysregulation of the circadian clock and causes altered expression and regulation of multiple circadian genes, including PER2, PER3, CRY1, BMAL1, and others

nodes.description: SMS is characterized by distinctive coarse facial features that progress with age.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: distinctive physical features (particularly coarse facial features that progress with age)

nodes.description: Constipation/obstipation is a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation of SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome: Clinical and behavioral characteristics in a large retrospective cohort.
evidence.snippet: We identified a high prevalence of obstipation (45%).

nodes.description: The common SMS deletion is mediated by non-allelic homologous recombination between flanking low-copy repeats (SMS-REPs) on 17p11.2, producing a recurrent ~3.5 Mb deletion. While RAI1 is the critical dosage-sensitive gene, haploinsufficiency of additional contiguous genes contributes to phenotypic variability and to features such as short stature and otolaryngologic findings.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: a heterozygous deletion of chromosome 17p11.2 that includes RAI1 or a heterozygous intragenic RAI1 pathogenic variant

nodes.description: Speech and language development is delayed in SMS, with expressive language typically more affected than receptive.

evidence.reference_title: Speech, Language, Hearing, and Otopathology Results From the International Smith-Magenis Syndrome Patient Registry.
evidence.snippet: is associated with delays in speech-language development, otopathology, and hearing loss

nodes.description: Global developmental delay is present, with infants showing hypotonia, feeding difficulties, and failure to thrive.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Smith-Magenis syndrome (SMS) is characterized by distinctive physical features (particularly coarse facial features that progress with age), developmental delay, cognitive impairment, behavioral abnormalities, sleep disturbances, and childhood-onset abdominal obesity.

nodes.description: Hearing loss is common in SMS, frequently associated with chronic otitis media and middle-ear dysfunction requiring pressure-equalization tubes.

evidence.reference_title: Speech, Language, Hearing, and Otopathology Results From the International Smith-Magenis Syndrome Patient Registry.
evidence.snippet: 35% of subjects had hearing loss, 66% had a history of otitis media, and 62% had received PE tubes.

nodes.description: Problems with executive function are common, including inattention, distractibility, hyperactivity, and impulsivity.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: problems with executive function, including inattention, distractibility, hyperactivity, and impulsivity

nodes.description: Childhood-to-adolescent onset obesity in SMS is linked to RAI1 dosage effects on hypothalamic feeding and satiety signaling, with hyperphagic/foraging behaviors. Dysfunction of the proximal melanocortin 4 receptor (MC4R) pathway has been hypothesized, although an open-label setmelanotide (MC4R agonist) trial did not significantly reduce body weight, suggesting the proximal MC4R pathway is not the predominant driver of SMS obesity.

evidence.reference_title: Investigation of setmelanotide, an MC4R agonist, for obesity in individuals with Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Obesity in people with SMS is believed partially due to dysfunction of the proximal melanocortin 4 receptor (MC4R) pathway.

evidence.reference_title: Investigation of setmelanotide, an MC4R agonist, for obesity in individuals with Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Results of this study do not suggest that dysfunction of the proximal MC4R pathway is the main etiology for obesity in people with SMS.

nodes.description: Most individuals with SMS function in the mild-to-moderate range of intellectual disability.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Most individuals function in the mild-to-moderate range of intellectual disability.

nodes.description: A hallmark of SMS is an inverted circadian rhythm of melatonin: most affected individuals secrete melatonin during the day rather than at night. This circadian dysregulation, downstream of RAI1 dosage effects on the molecular clock, underlies the characteristic sleep disturbance (early sleep onset and offset, frequent night awakenings, daytime sleepiness).

evidence.reference_title: Inversion of the circadian rhythm of melatonin in the Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: All children with SMS had a phase shift of their circadian rhythm of melatonin.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome results in disruption of CLOCK gene transcription and reveals an integral role for RAI1 in the maintenance of circadian rhythmicity.
evidence.snippet: An inverted melatonin rhythm (i.e., melatonin peaks during the day instead of at night) and associated sleep-phase disturbances in individuals with SMS

nodes.description: Stereotypic behaviors are common; the spasmodic upper-body squeeze or "self-hug" is highly associated with SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Among the stereotypic behaviors described, the spasmodic upper body squeeze or "self-hug" seems to be highly associated with SMS.

nodes.description: Infants with SMS have hypotonia, hyporeflexia, feeding difficulties, and failure to thrive.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Infants have feeding difficulties, failure to thrive, hypotonia, hyporeflexia, prolonged napping or need to be awakened for feeds, and generalized lethargy.

nodes.description: Overweight and obesity develop in most individuals with SMS, typically from later childhood and adolescence, often with hyperphagic/foraging behaviors.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome: Clinical and behavioral characteristics in a large retrospective cohort.
evidence.snippet: 60% of our patients older than 10 years were overweight.

nodes.description: Yanking out of fingernails and/or toenails (onychotillomania) is a distinctive self-injurious behavior in SMS.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: yanking fingernails and/or toenails (onychotillomania)

nodes.description: Ophthalmologic problems are highly prevalent in SMS — reported in 89% of a large European cohort — and include refractive errors, strabismus, and other eye findings; GeneReviews recommends annual ophthalmology evaluations.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome: Clinical and behavioral characteristics in a large retrospective cohort.
evidence.snippet: ophthalmological problems (89%)

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Annual otolaryngology, audiology, and ophthalmology evaluations.

nodes.description: A history of otitis media is highly prevalent in SMS, contributing to conductive hearing loss and the need for pressure-equalization tubes.

evidence.reference_title: Speech, Language, Hearing, and Otopathology Results From the International Smith-Magenis Syndrome Patient Registry.
evidence.snippet: 35% of subjects had hearing loss, 66% had a history of otitis media, and 62% had received PE tubes.

nodes.description: Overeating and hyperphagic/foraging behaviors are described in SMS and contribute to obesity.

evidence.reference_title: RAI1 gene mutations: mechanisms of Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: They usually have lower incidence of hypotonia and less cognitive impairment than those with 17p11.2 deletions but more frequently show the behavioral characteristics of the syndrome and overeating issues.

nodes.description: The unifying molecular cause of SMS is reduced dosage of RAI1, whether from a 17p11.2 deletion that removes one RAI1 allele or from an intragenic loss-of-function variant. RAI1 encodes a nuclear transcriptional regulator; haploinsufficiency disrupts a broad downstream transcriptional program, producing the neurodevelopmental and systemic phenotype.

biological_processes: Regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: Mutations in RAI1 associated with Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We identified dominant frameshift mutations leading to protein truncation in RAI1 in three individuals who have phenotypic features consistent with SMS but do not have 17p11.2 deletions

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome results in disruption of CLOCK gene transcription and reveals an integral role for RAI1 in the maintenance of circadian rhythmicity.
evidence.snippet: Data support RAI1 as a transcriptional regulator

evidence.reference_title: RAI1 gene mutations: mechanisms of Smith-Magenis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: RAI1 (or its homologs in animal models) acts as a transcriptional factor implicated in embryonic neurodevelopment, neuronal differentiation, cell growth and cell cycle regulation, bone and skeletal development, lipid and glucose metabolisms, behavioral functions, and circadian activity

nodes.description: Scoliosis is a recognized skeletal manifestation of SMS requiring orthopedic surveillance, reported in 43% of a large European cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis syndrome: Clinical and behavioral characteristics in a large retrospective cohort.
evidence.snippet: scoliosis (43%)

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Monitor for the development and/or progression of seizures and scoliosis.

nodes.description: Maladaptive behaviors include self-injurious behaviors such as self-hitting, self-biting, skin picking, polyembolokoilamania (inserting foreign objects into body orifices), and onychotillomania (nail pulling).

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Maladaptive behaviors include frequent outbursts / temper tantrums, attention-seeking behaviors, opposition, aggression, and self-injurious behaviors including self-hitting, self-biting, skin picking

nodes.description: Significant sleep disturbance is a hallmark of SMS, driven by the inverted circadian melatonin rhythm; it includes difficulty falling asleep, frequent and prolonged night awakenings, early morning waking, and excessive daytime sleepiness.

evidence.reference_title: Smith-Magenis Syndrome (GeneReviews)
evidence.snippet: Behavioral manifestations, including significant sleep disturbances, stereotypies, and maladaptive and self-injurious behaviors
